comments.text: I thought I'd share some experimental data on this problem. I don't have any other ambitions with it. In fact, these sums of divisors behave so erratically that I'll be amazed if someone can prove anything nontrivial rigorously and unconditionally. [EDIT: I was unnecessarily pessimistic. Terry Tao solved the second part under [1054] and there were further upgrades.] I entered the sizes of sets $D'_n:=D_n\setminus\cup_{m<n}D_m$ as OEIS sequence A387502. It is unclear what Erdős and Graham wanted to know about these numbers. There are many zeros in the sequence, i.e., many empty sets $D'_n$ (also see below), which can be conditionally deduced from (certain special cases of Schinzel's hypothesis), say, having infinitely primes $r$ of the form $r=pq+p+q$, $p<q$ primes, since then $D'_r=\emptyset$. On the other hand, the sets $D'_n$ can likely become arbitrarily large: the largest one I could find is $D'_{75600}$ of cardinality $24$. The very likely candidates are the numbers $n$ with large $\sigma(n)$. In reality, the record values of the cardinalities of $D'_n$ are attained for $n=2, 12, 36, 144, 336, 1320, 1980, 5040, 8400, 25200, 75600$. I also entered sizes of the unions $\cup_{m\leq n}D_m=\cup_{m\leq n}D'_m$ as OEIS sequence A387503. From it one can see that the first $1000$ sets $D'_n$ have average size $0.924$, the first $10000$ have average size $0.8697$, and I also computed that the first $100000$ sets $D'_n$ have average size $0.8349$. This should also hint again that a positive share of the sets $D'_n$ are empty. Regarding the behavior of the function $f$, the same problem is also mentioned in the book by Guy and attributed to Erdős. There is a slight difference in the book: that the number $1$ is always counted in as the smallest divisor, but effectively this makes no difference, and the function $f$ only gets shifted by $1$. It seems that Guy's book was a better-known reference, since the values of $f$ already appear as OEIS sequence A167485 (but again, note the indices shifted by $1$). Function $f$ is clearly undefined for $n=1,4$ (as the two smallest divisors $>1$ cannot add up to $1$ or $4$), and I'm not sure if it is known to be well-defined for all other $n$ (but experimentally it seems so). OEIS mentions this following from a certain stronger version of Goldbach's conjecture, but it would also follow from versions of Vingradov's theorem. For instance if sufficiently (but concretely) large odd $N$ are sums of distinct primes $p,q,r\in[x,x^2]$, then $N$ appears in the set $D_{pqr}$, which makes $f(N)$ finite (i.e., well-defined). The claim $f(N)=o(N)$ seems experimentally unlikely. In fact, $31.279\%$ of the numbers $N\leq100000$ even have $f(N)>100000$. Similarly, only less than $36\%$ of the ratios $f(N)/N$, for $5\leq N\leq10000$, are $\leq1$, only less than $27\%$ of them are $\leq0.9$, and only less than $6\%$ of them are $\leq0.5$: quite unlikely if one had $f(N)/N\to0$. A very easy observation (mentioned in the OEIS comments) is that $\limsup_{n\to\infty}\sigma(n)/n=\infty$ by taking $N=\sigma(n)-1$ implies $\liminf_{N\to\infty}f(N)/N=0$, so at least this is an unconditional but rather trivial result.
